title: Agent Threads: A Framework for Scaling AI Coding
description: Learn a mental model for measuring and scaling your AI coding productivity through thread-based engineering.

cards.title: Master Agent Threads
cards.body: Learn a powerful framework for measuring and scaling your AI coding productivity. You'll discover how to track progress, run parallel agents, and ship like the pros.

cards.title: The Growing Skill Gap
cards.body: Even top engineers like Andrej Karpathy feel behind. Agentic engineering is a new skill that needs new frameworks to measure progress.

source.author: IndyDevDan
source.description: Why even top engineers feel behind in the age of agents

cards.warn: If you don't measure your agent progress, you can't improve systematically

cards.title: What is a Thread?
cards.body: A thread is a unit of work over time driven by you and your agents. It has three parts:
bullets: Prompt: You show up at the beginning with instructions
Tool calls: Your agent does the work in the middle
Review: You show up at the end to validate results

analogy.text: Like directing a movie: you call "action," the actors perform, then you call "cut" and review the take

cards.title: Start Your First Conscious Thread
cards.body: Open your terminal and run a prompt consciously as a thread. Notice the tool call count when it finishes.
cards.code:
# Your prompt here
What is this codebase about?
cards.codeCaption: This starts a new thread - your agent will make tool calls to analyze the code

source.author: IndyDevDan
source.description: See a concrete example of starting and completing a thread

cards.title: The Key Insight: Tool Calls = Impact
cards.body: Tool calls roughly equal impact (assuming useful prompts). Pre-2023, you did the tool calls. Now agents do them for you.
bullets: Before: You wrote code, read files, made requests
Now: Agents do the work, you prompt and review
More tool calls = more work accomplished
cards.safe: This framework works across all AI coding tools - Claude Code, Cursor, etc.

cards.title: Quick Check
cards.question: In thread-based engineering, when do you show up?

options.text: Only at the beginning to write prompts
Throughout the entire process monitoring tool calls
Only at the end to check results

cards.correctFeedback: Exactly! You bookend the work - prompt at start, review at end, let agents handle the middle.
cards.wrongFeedback: Not quite. You show up at both the beginning (prompt) and end (review), while agents handle the tool calls in between.

cards.title: Level Up: Parallel Threads (P-threads)
cards.body: After mastering single threads, you can scale with parallel execution. Run multiple threads simultaneously across different terminals, git work trees, or sandboxes.

analogy.text: Like a circus director managing multiple rings at once - each performer works independently while you oversee the show

cards.title: Boris Cherny's 5-Terminal Setup
cards.body: Learn from the Claude Code creator: set up 5 parallel agents in numbered terminal tabs, just like Boris does.
cards.code:
# Terminal tabs 1-5
# Tab 1: Feature development
# Tab 2: Bug fixes
# Tab 3: Code review
# Tab 4: Documentation
# Tab 5: Testing

source.author: IndyDevDan
source.description: Boris Cherny's vanilla setup with 5 Claude Code instances

helpItems: Number your terminal tabs 1-5 for easy switching
Each tab handles a different type of work
Boris also runs 5-10 additional web Claude Code sessions

cards.title: Practice Parallel Reviews
cards.body: Spin up 3-5 parallel agent sessions on distinct tasks. Practice reviewing all of them in sequence to get comfortable with the workflow.
bullets: Open 3-5 separate terminals or IDE windows
Give each agent a different task (review, implement, test)
Let them work simultaneously
Review each one's output in sequence
cards.troubleshoot: If you feel overwhelmed, start with just 2 parallel threads and work your way up

cards.title: You're Scaling Up!
cards.body: You now understand thread-based thinking and parallel execution. You're already thinking like a senior agentic engineer!

cards.title: Advanced: Chained Threads
cards.body: Chain threads together for complex tasks. Each thread's output becomes the input for the next, creating a pipeline of work.
bullets: Break risky tasks (migrations, refactors) into phases
Complete and review each phase before the next
Each phase is its own thread with clear handoffs
cards.warn: Never chain threads for production work without reviewing each phase - one bad link breaks the whole chain

cards.title: Try a Chained Thread
cards.body: Break your next risky production task into a phase-by-phase plan. Only prompt the next phase after reviewing the previous one.
cards.code:
Phase 1: Analysis and planning
[REVIEW CHECKPOINT]
Phase 2: Implementation
[REVIEW CHECKPOINT]
Phase 3: Testing and validation
[REVIEW CHECKPOINT]
cards.codeCaption: Each phase is a complete thread with its own prompt → tool calls → review cycle

cards.title: Pro Move: Fusion Threads
cards.body: Send the same prompt to multiple agents, compare their outputs, then manually combine the strongest parts into one superior result.
bullets: Same prompt to 2-3 different agents (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.)
Compare their approaches and solutions
Cherry-pick the best parts from each
Combine into one optimized result

analogy.text: Like getting multiple expert opinions before surgery - each specialist brings unique insights

cards.title: Execute Your First Fusion Thread
cards.body: Try a fusion thread: send the same prompt to 2-3 agents, compare outputs, and manually combine the strongest parts.
helpItems: Use different tools: Claude Code, Cursor, ChatGPT, etc.
Look for different approaches and solutions
Create a hybrid solution using the best from each

source.author: IndyDevDan
source.description: See fusion threads in action with multiple agent outputs

cards.title: Knowledge Check
cards.question: What's the main benefit of measuring tool calls in your threads?

options.text: To minimize the number of API calls for cost savings
To track and improve your engineering productivity over time
To debug errors in your agent's performance
To compare different AI models objectively

cards.correctFeedback: Perfect! Tool calls = impact, so measuring them helps you track and improve your productivity systematically.
cards.wrongFeedback: Not quite. The main benefit is productivity tracking - tool calls roughly equal impact, so measuring them shows your improvement over time.

cards.title: How to Know You're Improving
cards.body: Track these metrics to measure your agentic engineering progress:
bullets: Thread complexity: Can you handle more tool calls per thread?
Parallel capacity: How many simultaneous threads can you manage?
Chain length: Can you successfully link multiple threads?
Fusion quality: Are your combined outputs better than individual ones?
cards.safe: Start simple and gradually increase complexity as you get comfortable with the framework

cards.title: Start Tracking Your Progress
cards.body: Begin consciously measuring your threads. Note tool call counts, successful parallel threads, and overall output quality.
bullets: Keep a simple log of your thread activities
Track tool calls per thread over time
Note your maximum successful parallel threads
Record wins and challenges for each approach
cards.code:
# Simple tracking format
Date: 2024-01-15
Threads: 3 parallel
Tool calls: 45 total
Success: Completed feature migration
Next: Try 4 parallel threads

cards.title: You're Now Thread-Ready!
cards.body: You've mastered the thread-based engineering framework! You can now measure progress, run parallel agents, chain complex work, and fuse outputs like a pro. You're ready to scale your AI coding impact.
